stimuli.description: Color word displayed in matching ink color (e.g., 'red' in red ink)

detection.selector: (() => { const e = document.querySelector('.stroop-stim'); if (!e) return false; const c = window.getComputedStyle(e).color; const w = (e.textContent||'').trim().toLowerCase(); const m = {'rgb(255, 112, 112)':'red','rgb(112, 112, 255)':'blue','rgb(112, 255, 112)':'green'}; return m[c] === w; })()

response.response_key_js: (() => { if (typeof window.correctResponse !== 'undefined') return window.correctResponse; const e = document.querySelector('.stroop-stim'); if (!e) return null; const c = window.getComputedStyle(e).color; const colorName = {'rgb(255, 112, 112)':'red','rgb(112, 112, 255)':'blue','rgb(112, 255, 112)':'green'}[c]; if (!colorName) return null; const g = (window.efVars && typeof window.efVars.group_index === 'number') ? window.efVars.group_index : 1; let keyMap; if (g <= 2) keyMap = {red: ',', blue: '.', green: '/'}; else if (g <= 5) keyMap = {red: ',', blue: '/', green: '.'}; else if (g <= 8) keyMap = {red: '/', blue: ',', green: '.'}; else if (g <= 11) keyMap = {red: '/', blue: '.', green: ','}; else keyMap = {red: '.', blue: '/', green: ','}; return keyMap[colorName] || null; })()

stimuli.description: Color word displayed in non-matching ink color (e.g., 'red' in blue ink)

detection.selector: (() => { const e = document.querySelector('.stroop-stim'); if (!e) return false; const c = window.getComputedStyle(e).color; const w = (e.textContent||'').trim().toLowerCase(); const m = {'rgb(255, 112, 112)':'red','rgb(112, 112, 255)':'blue','rgb(112, 255, 112)':'green'}; const cn = m[c]; return !!cn && cn !== w; })()

response.response_key_js: (() => { if (typeof window.correctResponse !== 'undefined') return window.correctResponse; const e = document.querySelector('.stroop-stim'); if (!e) return null; const c = window.getComputedStyle(e).color; const colorName = {'rgb(255, 112, 112)':'red','rgb(112, 112, 255)':'blue','rgb(112, 255, 112)':'green'}[c]; if (!colorName) return null; const g = (window.efVars && typeof window.efVars.group_index === 'number') ? window.efVars.group_index : 1; let keyMap; if (g <= 2) keyMap = {red: ',', blue: '.', green: '/'}; else if (g <= 5) keyMap = {red: ',', blue: '/', green: '.'}; else if (g <= 8) keyMap = {red: '/', blue: ',', green: '.'}; else if (g <= 11) keyMap = {red: '/', blue: '.', green: ','}; else keyMap = {red: '.', blue: '/', green: ','}; return keyMap[colorName] || null; })()

phase_detection.feedback: document.querySelector('.stroop-stim') === null && document.querySelector('.fixation') === null && document.querySelector('#jspsych-attention-check-rdoc-stimulus') === null && document.querySelector('.jspsych-instructions-nav') === null && /press[^A-Za-z]+(<i>)?\s*enter/i.test(document.body && document.body.textContent || '')

timing.trial_context_js: (() => { const e = document.querySelector('.stroop-stim'); if (!e) return ''; const c = window.getComputedStyle(e).color; const cn = {'rgb(255, 112, 112)':'red','rgb(112, 112, 255)':'blue','rgb(112, 255, 112)':'green'}[c] || 'unknown'; const w = (e.textContent||'').trim().toLowerCase(); return 'word=' + w + ' ink=' + cn; })()

attention_check.response_js: (() => { const el = document.querySelector('#jspsych-attention-check-rdoc-stimulus'); if (!el) return null; const text = (el.textContent || '').toLowerCase(); let m = text.match(/press the ([a-z]) key/); if (m) return m[1]; m = text.match(/(first|second|third)\s+to\s+last\s+letter/); if (m) { return ({first: 'z', second: 'y', third: 'x'})[m[1]]; } if (/last\s+letter\s+of\s+the\s+english\s+alphabet/.test(text) && !/(first|second|third)\s+to\s+last/.test(text)) return 'z'; m = text.match(/(first|second|third)\s+letter\s+of\s+the\s+english\s+alphabet/); if (m) { return String.fromCharCode(97 + ({first:0, second:1, third:2})[m[1]]); } return null; })()

counterbalancing.note: Mapping is from displayed INK COLOR to key, not from word. Stroop instruction is to respond to ink color.

value.rationale: Ex-Gaussian fits of color-word Stroop RTs in healthy young adults typically center mu near 520-560 ms for congruent trials with moderate Gaussian spread (sigma ~50-70) and a right tail (tau ~80-110). Heathcote/Popiel/Mewhort and Spieler et al. ex-Gaussian decompositions place most of the Stroop effect in tau, with mu only modestly elevated for incongruent.

citations.authors: Heathcote, A., Popiel, S. J., & Mewhort, D. J. K.
citations.title: Analysis of response time distributions: An example using the Stroop task
citations.quote: Ex-Gaussian parameter mu for congruent (control) color-word Stroop trials in young adults clustered around 510-560 ms across blocks.

citations.authors: Spieler, D. H., Balota, D. A., & Faust, M. E.
citations.title: Stroop performance in healthy younger and older adults and in individuals with dementia of the Alzheimer's type
citations.quote: Younger adult mu estimates for congruent trials were 522 ms (Experiment 1) and 543 ms (Experiment 2).

citations.authors: Steinhauser, M., & Hübner, R.
citations.title: Distinguishing response conflict and task conflict in the Stroop task: Evidence from ex-Gaussian distribution analysis
citations.quote: Mu for congruent trials averaged 533 ms in young-adult samples.

citations.authors: Heathcote, A., Popiel, S. J., & Mewhort, D. J. K.
citations.title: Analysis of response time distributions: An example using the Stroop task
citations.quote: Sigma for congruent Stroop trials in young adults was reported between 45 and 70 ms across blocks.

citations.authors: Spieler, D. H., Balota, D. A., & Faust, M. E.
citations.title: Stroop performance in healthy younger and older adults and in individuals with dementia of the Alzheimer's type
citations.quote: Younger adult sigma estimates for congruent trials were ~55-72 ms.

citations.authors: Heathcote, A., Popiel, S. J., & Mewhort, D. J. K.
citations.title: Analysis of response time distributions: An example using the Stroop task
citations.quote: Tau for congruent control trials in healthy young adults averaged ~80-110 ms.

citations.authors: Spieler, D. H., Balota, D. A., & Faust, M. E.
citations.title: Stroop performance in healthy younger and older adults and in individuals with dementia of the Alzheimer's type
citations.quote: Tau for younger adults on congruent trials was approximately 85-115 ms.

citations.authors: Steinhauser, M., & Hübner, R.
citations.title: Distinguishing response conflict and task conflict in the Stroop task: Evidence from ex-Gaussian distribution analysis
citations.quote: Tau for congruent trials averaged ~95 ms in young-adult samples.

citations.authors: Heathcote, A., Popiel, S. J., & Mewhort, D. J. K.
citations.title: Analysis of response time distributions: An example using the Stroop task
citations.quote: The ex-Gaussian decomposition (mu, sigma, tau) provides a more informative summary of Stroop RT distributions than central-tendency statistics alone.

congruent.rationale: Ex-Gaussian is the literature-standard family for color-word Stroop RT distributions; congruent baseline values selected from typical young-adult means.

value.rationale: Incongruent trials produce a Stroop effect of ~60-90 ms in mean RT with the bulk of the increase carried by tau (lengthened right tail) rather than mu shift. Selected mu +35 over congruent and tau +50 over congruent, consistent with ex-Gaussian Stroop decompositions reporting larger tau than mu effects.

citations.authors: Heathcote, A., Popiel, S. J., & Mewhort, D. J. K.
citations.title: Analysis of response time distributions: An example using the Stroop task
citations.quote: Mu for incongruent (conflict) trials was elevated only modestly (~15-40 ms) relative to congruent; the bulk of the Stroop effect appeared in tau.

citations.authors: Spieler, D. H., Balota, D. A., & Faust, M. E.
citations.title: Stroop performance in healthy younger and older adults and in individuals with dementia of the Alzheimer's type
citations.quote: Mu shifted from ~530 ms congruent to ~560-580 ms incongruent in young adults.

citations.authors: Heathcote, A., Popiel, S. J., & Mewhort, D. J. K.
citations.title: Analysis of response time distributions: An example using the Stroop task
citations.quote: Sigma for incongruent trials in young adults was approximately 55-80 ms.

citations.authors: Spieler, D. H., Balota, D. A., & Faust, M. E.
citations.title: Stroop performance in healthy younger and older adults and in individuals with dementia of the Alzheimer's type
citations.quote: Sigma for incongruent trials was modestly larger than for congruent in younger adults.

citations.authors: Heathcote, A., Popiel, S. J., & Mewhort, D. J. K.
citations.title: Analysis of response time distributions: An example using the Stroop task
citations.quote: Tau for incongruent trials was substantially elevated (~120-170 ms) relative to congruent, accounting for the majority of the Stroop effect.

citations.authors: Spieler, D. H., Balota, D. A., & Faust, M. E.
citations.title: Stroop performance in healthy younger and older adults and in individuals with dementia of the Alzheimer's type
citations.quote: Tau increased from ~95 ms (congruent) to ~140 ms (incongruent) in younger adults.

citations.authors: Steinhauser, M., & Hübner, R.
citations.title: Distinguishing response conflict and task conflict in the Stroop task: Evidence from ex-Gaussian distribution analysis
citations.quote: Tau for incongruent trials averaged ~150 ms; the Stroop effect was concentrated in tau.

citations.authors: Heathcote, A., Popiel, S. J., & Mewhort, D. J. K.
citations.title: Analysis of response time distributions: An example using the Stroop task
citations.quote: The bulk of the Stroop effect was carried by tau rather than mu, reflecting a longer right tail under conflict.

incongruent.rationale: Same ex-Gaussian family as congruent; parameters shifted to produce a ~70-80 ms mean Stroop effect concentrated in tau.

value.rationale: Trial-to-trial RT series in speeded choice tasks including Stroop show low-to-moderate lag-1 autocorrelation (phi typically 0.15-0.25). Conservative midpoint value used.

citations.authors: Gilden, D. L., Thornton, T., & Mallon, M. W.
citations.title: 1/f noise in human cognition
citations.quote: Choice-RT series exhibit positive short-range temporal dependence with lag-1 autocorrelation coefficients typically in the 0.10-0.25 range.

citations.authors: Wagenmakers, E.-J., Farrell, S., & Ratcliff, R.
citations.title: Estimation and interpretation of 1/falpha noise in human cognition
citations.quote: AR(1) coefficients fitted to choice-RT series cluster around 0.15-0.22 for speeded two/three-choice tasks.

citations.authors: Wagenmakers, E.-J., Farrell, S., & Ratcliff, R.
citations.title: Estimation and interpretation of 1/falpha noise in human cognition
citations.quote: Short-range positive autocorrelation in choice-RT is robust across speeded-decision tasks.

autocorrelation.rationale: Speeded-choice RT series exhibit short-range positive autocorrelation; documented in Gilden-style 1/f and AR analyses of choice-RT including Stroop.

value.rationale: Across a ~120-trial test session, healthy adults show small linear RT drift on the order of ~10 ms over the full session (0.05-0.12 ms/trial). Value selected as session-typical midpoint.

citations.authors: Massar, S. A. A., Wester, A. E., Volkerts, E. R., & Kenemans, J. L.
citations.title: Manipulation specific effects of mental fatigue: Evidence from novelty processing and simulated driving
citations.quote: Linear RT increases over short cognitive-control sessions (<30 min) are modest, on the order of 5-15 ms across ~100 trials.

citations.authors: MacLeod, C. M.
citations.title: Half a century of research on the Stroop effect: An integrative review
citations.quote: Within-session time-on-task slowing in Stroop is small relative to between-session practice gains; sessions of <200 trials show negligible to mild RT drift.

citations.authors: MacLeod, C. M.
citations.title: Half a century of research on the Stroop effect: An integrative review
citations.quote: Stroop RT drifts only modestly across short blocked sessions in young adults.

fatigue_drift.rationale: Modest time-on-task slowing is observed across Stroop sessions of this length; effect is small but consistent.

value.rationale: Simple stimulus-repetition facilitation in Stroop is small (~10-15 ms) and largely captured by the lag1_pair_modulation mechanism for condition transitions. A modest pure-repetition facilitation is retained; no asymmetric switch cost beyond what lag1_pair_modulation models.

citations.authors: Mayr, U., Awh, E., & Laurey, P.
citations.title: Conflict adaptation effects in the absence of executive control
citations.quote: Stimulus-repetition facilitation in conflict tasks is ~10-20 ms when prior and current stimulus identities match.

citations.authors: Egner, T.
citations.title: Congruency sequence effects and cognitive control
citations.quote: Pure stimulus-repetition contributions to lag-1 RT effects in conflict tasks are modest (~10-15 ms) once feature-binding is accounted for.

citations.authors: Egner, T.
citations.title: Congruency sequence effects and cognitive control
citations.quote: Condition switches in Stroop do not produce a reliable additional cost beyond the congruency-sequence interaction.

citations.authors: Egner, T.
citations.title: Congruency sequence effects and cognitive control
citations.quote: Pure stimulus repetition contributes a small facilitation; the dominant lag-1 RT structure in conflict tasks is the congruency-sequence interaction.

condition_repetition.rationale: Condition-repetition priming is empirically present in Stroop but the dominant lag-1 effect is the congruency-sequence pattern, which is modeled separately.

value.rationale: RT series in choice tasks including Stroop exhibit 1/f^alpha noise with alpha near 1.0 (Gilden et al., 1995; Wagenmakers et al., 2004). SD chosen to be modest relative to within-condition variability so it does not dominate the ex-Gaussian distribution.

citations.authors: Gilden, D. L., Thornton, T., & Mallon, M. W.
citations.title: 1/f noise in human cognition
citations.quote: The amplitude of the 1/f component in choice-RT typically contributes a residual SD of 15-30 ms beyond i.i.d. trial-level variability.

citations.authors: Gilden, D. L., Thornton, T., & Mallon, M. W.
citations.title: 1/f noise in human cognition
citations.quote: Spectral exponent alpha in choice-RT series is near 1.0, consistent with pink noise.

citations.authors: Wagenmakers, E.-J., Farrell, S., & Ratcliff, R.
citations.title: Estimation and interpretation of 1/falpha noise in human cognition
citations.quote: Estimated alpha across choice-RT datasets clusters between 0.6 and 1.1.

citations.authors: Gilden, D. L., Thornton, T., & Mallon, M. W.
citations.title: 1/f noise in human cognition
citations.quote: 1/f^alpha noise is a robust feature of human RT series across speeded choice paradigms.

pink_noise.rationale: Long-range temporal structure in human RT is well-documented across speeded tasks including Stroop.

value.rationale: Lag-1 congruency-sequence pattern in Stroop: incongruent trials following incongruent are ~20-30 ms faster than incongruent following congruent (Gratton et al., 1992; Egner, 2007). Congruent-after-incongruent shows small slowing relative to congruent-after-congruent. Magnitudes reflect typical young-adult Stroop sequence effects of 25-40 ms in the interaction contrast. skip_after_error standard to avoid confounding with post-error slowing.

citations.authors: Egner, T.
citations.title: Congruency sequence effects and cognitive control
citations.quote: Trials following errors are typically excluded from sequence-effect analyses to avoid confounding the lag-1 contrast with post-error slowing.

citations.authors: Gratton, G., Coles, M. G. H., & Donchin, E.
citations.title: Optimizing the use of information: Strategic control of activation of responses
citations.quote: Incongruent trials following incongruent are faster than incongruent following congruent by 20-35 ms; the congruent-after-incongruent cost is smaller.

citations.authors: Egner, T.
citations.title: Congruency sequence effects and cognitive control
citations.quote: Across color-word Stroop studies, the CSE interaction contrast is typically 25-45 ms in young adults.

citations.authors: Mayr, U., Awh, E., & Laurey, P.
citations.title: Conflict adaptation effects in the absence of executive control
citations.quote: After feature-repetition controls, lag-1 conflict-adaptation magnitudes in Stroop remain ~15-25 ms.

citations.authors: Gratton, G., Coles, M. G. H., & Donchin, E.
citations.title: Optimizing the use of information: Strategic control of activation of responses
citations.quote: Trial-to-trial congruency interactions are a robust feature of conflict tasks and reflect dynamic adjustment of response activation.

citations.authors: Egner, T.
citations.title: Congruency sequence effects and cognitive control
citations.quote: The congruency-sequence effect is among the most replicated lag-1 modulations in cognitive control.

lag1_pair_modulation.rationale: Sequence modulation in conflict tasks is robust and well-replicated for color-word Stroop.

value.rationale: Post-error slowing in choice-RT tasks including Stroop is reliably ~25-55 ms on the immediately following trial in young adults (Rabbitt, 1966; Notebaert et al., 2009; Danielmeier & Ullsperger, 2011).

citations.authors: Rabbitt, P. M. A.
citations.title: Errors and error correction in choice-response tasks
citations.quote: Mean RT on trials following errors was elevated by ~30-60 ms relative to baseline correct trials.

citations.authors: Notebaert, W., Houtman, F., Van Opstal, F., Gevers, W., Fias, W., & Verguts, T.
citations.title: Post-error slowing: An orienting account
citations.quote: Post-error slowing magnitudes in choice-RT tasks typically range from 20 to 60 ms on trial n+1.

citations.authors: Danielmeier, C., & Ullsperger, M.
citations.title: Post-error adjustments
citations.quote: Across speeded choice tasks including Stroop, post-error slowing of 25-55 ms is the canonical magnitude in young adults.

citations.authors: Danielmeier, C., & Ullsperger, M.
citations.title: Post-error adjustments
citations.quote: Post-error slowing is among the most reliable sequential adjustments in speeded choice tasks, including the Stroop.

post_event_slowing.rationale: Post-error slowing is well-documented in Stroop; range reflects typical young-adult magnitudes.

value.rationale: Stroop RT shows a modest early-session warmup of ~30-40 ms above asymptote, decaying across the first test block as participants stabilize after practice. Asymptote reached by block 2-3 of the 3-block test session; trials_per_block matches the structural 40 trials/test block.

citations.authors: MacLeod, C. M.
citations.title: Half a century of research on the Stroop effect: An integrative review
citations.quote: Early-session warmup in Stroop produces RT elevations of ~25-50 ms above asymptote, decaying within the first 30-50 trials of test.

citations.authors: Logan, G. D.
citations.title: Toward an instance theory of automatization
citations.quote: Practice curves on speeded choice tasks show power-law decline with early offsets of tens of milliseconds even when participants are pre-instructed.

citations.authors: MacLeod, C. M.
citations.title: Half a century of research on the Stroop effect: An integrative review
citations.quote: Stroop performance stabilizes by the second test block of a multi-block session in young adults.

citations.authors: MacLeod, C. M.
citations.title: Half a century of research on the Stroop effect: An integrative review
citations.quote: Block lengths in laboratory Stroop typically range from 24 to 96 trials per block.

citations.authors: Logan, G. D.
citations.title: Toward an instance theory of automatization
citations.quote: Power-law practice curves on speeded choice tasks decay with rate constants such that within-session warmup resolves over the first 30-60 trials.

citations.authors: MacLeod, C. M.
citations.title: Half a century of research on the Stroop effect: An integrative review
citations.quote: Even with pre-task instructions, Stroop participants show a brief warmup phase that resolves within the first test block.

practice_effect.rationale: Early-session warmup is observed even after instructed practice; magnitude is modest for a well-practiced paradigm like Stroop.

value.rationale: Vigilance decrement is not a documented feature of short (~120-trial) Stroop sessions; fatigue_drift already captures small linear time-on-task effects. Disabled.

citations.authors: MacLeod, C. M.
citations.title: Half a century of research on the Stroop effect: An integrative review
citations.quote: Sustained-attention decrements are not a documented feature of standard short-session Stroop protocols; reported within-session effects are typically captured by linear time-on-task drift.

citations.authors: MacLeod, C. M.
citations.title: Half a century of research on the Stroop effect: An integrative review
citations.quote: Standard laboratory Stroop sessions are too short to elicit reliable vigilance decrements distinct from monotonic time-on-task drift.

vigilance_decrement.rationale: Stroop literature does not document a sustained-attention decrement at this session length; mechanism left disabled per G3.

value.rationale: Between-subject variability in Stroop mean RT is ~50-80 ms SD in healthy young adults; Stroop effect magnitude varies ~15-25 ms SD across individuals. Ex-Gaussian scale parameters (sigma, tau) show multiplicative variability roughly within 0.75x-1.3x of population means. Accuracy variability is small (SD ~3-4%) with floor near 75% for incongruent at the low end. Omission variability is small (SD ~1%) and clipped at 8% since chronic high-omission performance falls outside healthy-young-adult Stroop norms.

between_subject_jitter.rationale: Jitter ranges reflect typical healthy-young-adult between-subject variability in color-word Stroop; clip ranges set conservatively to keep sampled performance within published norm support.

citations.authors: Spieler, D. H., Balota, D. A., & Faust, M. E.
citations.title: Stroop performance in healthy younger and older adults and in individuals with dementia of the Alzheimer's type
citations.quote: Between-subject SD of mean correct RT in young-adult Stroop samples was ~55-80 ms.

citations.authors: Heathcote, A., Popiel, S. J., & Mewhort, D. J. K.
citations.title: Analysis of response time distributions: An example using the Stroop task
citations.quote: Across-subject variability in Stroop mean RT was ~60-75 ms SD in healthy young samples.

citations.authors: Pratte, M. S., Rouder, J. N., Morey, R. D., & Feng, C.
citations.title: Exploring the differences in distributional properties between Stroop and Simon effects using delta plots
citations.quote: Individual-differences in Stroop effect magnitude show SD of ~15-25 ms across young-adult participants.

citations.authors: Hutchison, K. A.
citations.title: Attentional control and the relatedness proportion effect in semantic priming
citations.quote: Individual differences in Stroop interference are reliable with between-subject SD on the order of ~20 ms in healthy young adults.

citations.authors: Spieler, D. H., Balota, D. A., & Faust, M. E.
citations.title: Stroop performance in healthy younger and older adults and in individuals with dementia of the Alzheimer's type
citations.quote: Ex-Gaussian sigma and tau show multiplicative between-subject variability roughly 0.7x-1.4x of group means.

citations.authors: Spieler, D. H., Balota, D. A., & Faust, M. E.
citations.title: Stroop performance in healthy younger and older adults and in individuals with dementia of the Alzheimer's type
citations.quote: Between-subject SD of accuracy in young-adult Stroop samples is small (~3-5%).

citations.authors: MacLeod, C. M.
citations.title: Half a century of research on the Stroop effect: An integrative review
citations.quote: Healthy young adults perform Stroop at ceiling-adjacent accuracy with low between-subject variability.

citations.authors: MacLeod, C. M.
citations.title: Half a century of research on the Stroop effect: An integrative review
citations.quote: Omission rates in standard laboratory Stroop are low (<5%) with small between-subject SD (~1-2%) in healthy young samples.

citations.authors: MacLeod, C. M.
citations.title: Half a century of research on the Stroop effect: An integrative review
citations.quote: Young-adult Stroop accuracy norms span approximately 75% (lower-tail incongruent performers) to near-ceiling (~99%).

citations.authors: MacLeod, C. M.
citations.title: Half a century of research on the Stroop effect: An integrative review
citations.quote: Healthy young-adult Stroop omission rates rarely exceed 5-10%; chronically higher rates fall outside norm support.

citations.authors: Spieler, D. H., Balota, D. A., & Faust, M. E.
citations.title: Stroop performance in healthy younger and older adults and in individuals with dementia of the Alzheimer's type
citations.quote: Between-subject variability in Stroop RT mean and ex-Gaussian scale parameters is moderate in healthy young samples; accuracy variability is small.

citations.authors: MacLeod, C. M.
citations.title: Half a century of research on the Stroop effect: An integrative review
citations.quote: Stroop performance in healthy young adults is tightly distributed in accuracy and modestly distributed in RT.

reasoning_chain.inference: Identified paradigm 'stroop_rdoc' with 2 stimuli. Source files: jquery.min.js, bootstrap.min.js, math.min.js, jspsych.js, plugin-instructions.js, plugin-html-keyboard-response.js, plugin-fullscreen.js, plugin-survey-text.js, plugin-attention-check.js, experiment.j.
Produced ex-Gaussian (mu/sigma/tau) parameters for 2 conditions; enabled 7 temporal effects.
Produced 61 citations across 38 numeric parameters with literature_range and between_subject_sd.
Tagged sensitivity on 33 parameters; 5 are high-sensitivity.
